era_context: The early 10th century CE continues to be characterized by political fragmentation and state consolidation across Eurasia. The Ottonian dynasty is establishing itself in East Francia. The Islamic world remains divided among competing caliphates and regional dynasties. East Asia is in the midst of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period, with the Liao dynasty recently established. Southeast Asia sees the continued development of the Khmer Empire. This is an era of military competition, religious expansion, and the gradual development of feudal and bureaucratic systems.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish sphere (sparse documentation)

events.title: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts; Byzantine consolidation in the Mediterranean
events.description: In 915 CE, the Byzantine Empire under Constantine VII was engaged in ongoing naval conflicts with Arab raiders and pirates in the Mediterranean. The Byzantine navy was working to secure trade routes and protect coastal settlements from Arab attacks. These conflicts were part of the broader struggle for control of the Mediterranean that had characterized the 9th and 10th centuries. Byzantine victories in naval warfare contributed to the empire's consolidation of power in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Byzantine naval history

events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts were ongoing throughout this period, but specific events for the year 915 are not always clearly documented. The general pattern of Byzantine naval activity is well-attested.
cross_references: Part of broader Byzantine-Arab conflicts (7th–10th centuries)
Contemporary with Byzantine consolidation in the Mediterranean

events.title: Later Liang dynasty continues; military competition in northern China
events.description: In 915 CE, the Later Liang dynasty under Emperor Taizong (r. 912–923) continued to rule northern China amid ongoing fragmentation and military competition. The dynasty faced pressure from rival states, particularly the Later Tang and the newly established Liao dynasty. The Later Liang period was characterized by military instability, with power concentrated in the hands of military governors and eunuchs. The Later Liang would fall to the Later Tang in 923.
key_figures: Zhu Youzhen / Emperor Taizong of Later Liang (r. 912–923)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Official histories of the Five Dynasties

events.certainty_note: The Later Liang dynasty's existence and general chronology are well-established. The year 915 falls within Emperor Taizong's reign.
cross_references: Part of Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period (907–979)
Led to Later Tang conquest in 923-001

events.title: Liao dynasty consolidation under Abaoji; Khitan expansion
events.description: The Liao dynasty (916–1125), founded by the Khitan people under Abaoji (Emperor Taizu of Liao, r. 916–926), was in its early years of consolidation in 915 CE (or just before its formal establishment in 916). Abaoji was unifying the Khitan tribes and establishing a state that would rival and eventually dominate parts of northern China. The Liao dynasty developed a sophisticated dual administrative system that combined Khitan and Chinese elements. The establishment of the Liao dynasty marked a significant shift in the balance of power in East Asia.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Liao dynasty is dated to 916 CE, so the year 915 falls just before the formal establishment. However, Abaoji's consolidation of Khitan power was ongoing in 915. The exact chronology of events in 915 is less certain than the formal founding date of 916.
cross_references: Led to Liao expansion and dominance in northern China (10th–11th centuries)
Contemporary with Later Liang and other Five Dynasties

events.title: Samanid Empire consolidation in Central Asia and Persia
events.description: The Samanid Empire (819–999) was in a period of consolidation and cultural flourishing in 915 CE. Under rulers like Nasr II (r. 914–943), the Samanids established a sophisticated bureaucracy, patronized scholars and poets, and controlled major trade routes. The Samanids represented a revival of Persian cultural and political identity within the Islamic world and were known for their support of the Persian language and literature. Their capital at Bukhara became a major center of learning and culture.
events.certainty_note: The Samanid Empire's existence and chronology are well-attested in Islamic historical sources. Nasr II's reign is securely dated to 914–943.
cross_references: Part of broader Samanid Empire period (819–999)

events.title: Fatimid Caliphate consolidation in North Africa
events.description: The Fatimid Caliphate, founded in 909 CE, was consolidating its power in North Africa in 915 CE. The Fatimids claimed descent from Fatima, daughter of the Prophet Muhammad, and represented a Shi'ite challenge to the Sunni Abbasid Caliphate. By 915, the Fatimids controlled much of the Maghreb and were beginning to expand their naval power in the Mediterranean. Their establishment of a rival caliphate marked a major schism in the Islamic world.
events.certainty_note: The Fatimid Caliphate's existence and general chronology are well-attested in Islamic historical sources. The year 915 falls within the reign of Abdallah al-Mahdi.
cross_references: Led to expansion into Egypt in 969-001

events.title: Khmer Empire; continued temple construction and cultural development
events.description: In 915 CE, the Khmer Empire was in a period of cultural and architectural flourishing. The period saw continued construction of temples and hydraulic infrastructure, including reservoirs (barays) that supported intensive agriculture and population growth. The Khmer developed sophisticated irrigation systems and integrated Hindu and Buddhist religious traditions. The period is marked by the development of a distinctive Khmer architectural style that would reach its peak at Angkor Wat in the 12th century.
key_figures: Khmer ruler (identity uncertain due to disputed chronology)
events.certainty_note: Khmer chronology for this period is based on epigraphic evidence and Chinese records, which are not always precise for specific years. The identity of the reigning Khmer ruler in 915 is uncertain due to disputed dating of Yasovarman I's reign.
cross_references: Part of Khmer Empire's classical period (9th–13th centuries)
Led to Angkor Wat construction in 12th century

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed for 915 CE. However, the exact chronology of the Khmer Empire remains disputed, and the identity of the reigning Khmer ruler in 915 is uncertain. The Liao dynasty is formally dated to 916, so events in 915 fall just before its establishment, though Abaoji's consolidation of Khitan power was ongoing.
historiographic_note: The year 915 CE is moderately well-documented for Western Europe, the Byzantine Empire, and the Islamic world, with good documentation for East Asia (particularly the pre-establishment period of the Liao dynasty). Byzantine sources on Constantine VII are abundant. Islamic sources are rich but often written centuries after the events. Chinese sources on the Five Dynasties are comprehensive. Southeast Asian history relies heavily on epigraphic evidence, which can be difficult to date precisely. The surviving sources are biased toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Court-sponsored chronicles dominate, potentially obscuring alternative perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Byzantine naval consolidation led to the empire's dominance in the Mediterranean
Later Liang's continued instability led to its conquest by Later Tang in 923
Abaoji's consolidation of Khitan power led to the formal establishment of the Liao dynasty in 916
Abaoji's consolidation of Khitan power was contemporary with Later Liang's rule in northern China
Samanid consolidation was contemporary with the rise of the Buyid dynasty
